original_abstract: During the hybrid armed conflict in which Russia became involved — the Special Military Operation — the medical service of the Armed Forces faced a number of challenges. This review analyzes foreign publications indexed in PubMed® concerning the conditions and factors affecting the activities of the medical services of armed forces, primarily those of NATO countries. It was revealed that a limiting factor for operational effectiveness is the staffing levels and qualitative composition of medical personnel, their preparedness to provide care for the specific pathologies of wartime, and maintaining these competencies in an up-to-date state. Important conditions for successful provision of medical care are preserving the integrity of medical facilities during targeted attacks on them, and the ability to use infrastructure in hostile or newly occupied territories. Prehospital care serves as a limiting factor in reducing lethality, with the main causes being fatal hemorrhages and head injuries from mine-blast trauma. Proper tourniquet application, rapid evacuation, and blood transfusions make the greatest contribution to reducing prehospital mortality. Among casualties, those with limb wounds are most significant, as they subsequently create the greatest social burden on the state, exceeding the “years lived with disability” parameter for all other disease classes, including oncological and cardiovascular diseases. In modern conflicts, the most dangerous in terms of lethality are mine-blast injuries (61.4–83.5%) and head injuries (20.9–59.0%), and in terms of subsequent disease burden, limb injuries (45.7%) constitute an absolute majority and are the point of focus for the main efforts of the medical services of the warring states’ armed forces. At the same time, there are no unified approaches regarding the place of application of qualified and specialized care among countries.
humanized_abstract: Overcoming traumatic injuries in the context of the Special Military Operation and lessons learned for the development of the Armed Forces’ medical service The hybrid war into which Russia was drawn in the course of the Special Military Operation in Ukraine has posed a whole set of challenges to the Armed Forces’ medical service. This review of articles in PubMed® on the experience of the armed forces’ medical services of NATO countries in dealing with trauma and factors affecting their ability to deliver medical care in wartime conditions reveals the factors affecting the armed forces’ ability to deliver medical care to soldiers and potentially civilians in peacetime and during the implementation of armed conflicts and in the transition to peacetime. These factors are as follows: — sufficient number and high qualification of medical personnel ready to treat casualties with typical traumatic injuries; — ongoing professional training of doctors, optimisation of their knowledge and skills; — the use of medical facilities with the least possible loss of their structural integrity in the event of enemy air raids, as well as using the existing infrastructure on occupied or enemy territory; — prehospital care and the prevention of fatal injuries; — high effectiveness of medical care provided in the immediate period following a traumatic injury and prior to the arrival of a medical crew in the event of a potentially fatal injury — haemorrhage and mine blast-induced traumatic brain injury; — timely application of a tourniquet and evacuation of a casualty with a traumatic injury as well as blood transfusion, which significantly improve the survival rate of a casualty with a potentially fatal prehospital traumatic injury; — optimal organisation of surgical care for large numbers of casualties with limb injuries, taking into account the high burden on the state of the years lived with disability (YLD) associated with these injuries, which exceed those for all other diseases, including cancer and cardiovascular disease. Mine-blast traumatic brain injuries and limb injuries are the most dangerous in terms of the number of fatalities and burden of diseases in modern armed conflicts. These types of injuries are the main focus of the efforts of the Armed Forces’ medical service and they are a key factor in determining the structure and volume of military medical resources and their location. It is also important to consider the issue of the location of the armed forces’ military medical facilities and their services and the role of military transport aircraft in the implementation of a system of casualty evacuation in the Armed Forces, which should be harmonised in peacetime with the national system for casualty evacuation and, during armed conflicts, should be as flexible as possible to meet the evolving challenges associated with the implementation of such operations. There are no unified approaches to the Armed Forces’ military medical services and their structure and operational activities among countries, which may complicate their cooperation in peacetime in the event of an attack on a state by an enemy with a highly developed armed forces structure.
